content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)'. International difference-in-differences evidence indicates that delaying selection from age 10–12 to age 14 would likely reduce Hungary's socioeconomic achievement gap by approximately 0.15–0.25 SD (based on Polish comparable gains), but this would require compensatory instructional resources and teacher supply that Hungary currently lacks in demonstrable quantity.
opportunities: Shrinks the selective track's footprint without requiring statutory redefinition of the secondary-education age boundary, potentially avoiding the felmenő-rendszer and ~1-year lead-time obligations that a structural reform would trigger.

Aligns policy with the demographic reality that the exam's original rationing function is eroding, as evidenced by falling 8-year-track applications (-14% year-on-year) alongside flat overall enrolment.
strengths: Difference-in-differences evidence from 75 countries/regions over 20 years shows early tracking increases test-score dispersion across PISA, PIRLS, and TIMSS cycles, though effect sizes vary substantially by study pair.

Early-tracking countries show larger inequality increases between primary and secondary school than late-tracking countries; Germany (+0.71), Greece (+0.30) and Czech Republic (+0.25) contrast with Turkey (−0.63), New Zealand (−0.50), Canada (−0.32).
threats: Reversal requires legislative repeal since score thresholds are maintainer-level administrative practice rather than statute, offering more procedural friction than S1's decree-based mechanism, but a parliamentary majority change during the multi-year rollout still poses a significant reversal risk given the Fidesz framing of any selection-related change and the government's own pattern of rapidly reversing predecessor reforms.

Church and foundation maintainers, protected by Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior operating conditions, may simply expand to absorb displaced demand, neutralizing the intended contraction and creating a policy-credibility problem.
weaknesses: Whether the Polish 1999 delayed-tracking reform's effect sizes (0.25–0.28 SD) would replicate in Hungary given different demographic composition (Roma representation, migration patterns) and school-system institutional differences.

The causal attribution of Hungary's 53-point socioeconomic achievement gap to early selection versus other structural factors (catchment segregation, resource allocation within schools, teacher quality distribution, out-of-school learning inequality).
